issue_title: [DNS] Help for 'az network dns record-set delete' refers to zone instead of record set
issue_body:
Consider:

```
C:\Users\jonatul>az network dns record-set delete -h

Command
    az network dns record-set delete: Deletes a record set within a DNS zone.

Arguments
    --name -n           [Required]: The name of the record set, relative to the name of the zone.
    --resource-group -g [Required]: Name of resource group.
    --type -t           [Required]: The type of DNS records in the record set.  Allowed values: A,
                                    AAAA, CNAME, MX, NS, PTR, SRV, TXT.
    --zone-name -z      [Required]: The name of the zone.
    --if-match                    : Defines the If-Match condition. The delete operation will be
                                    performed only if the ETag of the zone on the server matches
                                    this value.
    --if-none-match               : Defines the If-None-Match condition. The delete operation will
                                    be performed only if the ETag of the zone on the server does not
                                    match this value.
```

The references above to 'zone' under `--if-match` and `--if-none-match` should actually refer to record sets (looks like a copy + paste error).

For '--if-match', please can we simply use the text directly from the [DNS Swagger](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/master/arm-dns/2016-04-01/swagger/dns.json)? I think it is clearer: "The etag of the record set. Omit this value to always delete the current record set. Specify the last-seen etag value to prevent accidentally deleting any concurrent changes."

issue_title: [Storage] Service Stats Methods Return Poor Error if SKU is not RAGRS
issue_body:
Calls to the `get_{service}_service_stats` methods in the Python SDK work only with RAGRS enabled storage accounts. If you use these commands with other storage account types, you simply get a series of HTTPS connection failure messages.

The service should query the storage account specified in the connection string and respond with an appropriate error message if the storage account type is incorrect.

from azure.common.client_factory import get_client_from_auth_file
from azure.mgmt.compute import ComputeManagementClient

```

issue_title: bad ESRI sources yield 0 row CSV files that don't look like errors
issue_body:
In a [run I did last night](http://com.somebits.oafull.s3-website-us-east-1.amazonaws.com/runs/1421723433.179/index.html) 27 of 578 sources resulted in a 23 byte out.csv file, something that just contains the headers. All of these sources have the same problem: "Downloaded 0 ESRI features" and a cache.json file that is an empty FeatureCollection.

One problem is that these ESRI sources aren't being processed correctly. It'd be nice to look at why. It could be the service is just broken in which case it'd be nice to signal that as a download error somehow.

The other problem is that the result collector should probably flag this kind of an output as an error. Maybe some fail safe in the reporting code that if the CSV file is too small it's assumed to be an error?

This is just a nice-to-have; it's pretty obvious to the user something went wrong. Here's the source list:

us-ia-polk us-pa-beaver us-ia-linn us-mn-polk us-mn-yellow_medicine us-mn-wadena us-va-roanoke us-tx-hurst us-in-madison us-mo-barry us-ga-gordon us-ct-avon us-nv-nye us-nv-lander us-nv-henderson us-tx-north_richland_hills us-mo-st_louis_county us-tx-colleyville us-tx-dallas us-va-fluvanna us-il-tazewell us-ar us-ct-lyme us-tx-keller us-mi-muskegon us-tn-memphis us-fl-alachua

issue_body:
## Long story short

I recently came up with a way of doing websocket masking that is faster than the current implementation. (please see https://www.willmcgugan.com/blog/tech/post/speeding-up-websockets-60x/). I'd be happy to create a PR, if you think it would be worth updating.

rm .install-deps
    rm: cannot remove '.install-deps': No such file or directory

These two lines:

        rm .install-deps
        rm .develop

should have a `-f` or `-rf` flag added to avoid such errors.

We just ran into this due to: github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/1954.  Here until we have a resolution to that issue.  To work around this we'd have to disable read_timeout, and re-impl read() to have a timeout per chunk.

We need to support `body.set_socket_timeout` API like in botocor:

``` 
 257         response = self.client.get_object(
 258             Bucket=self.bucket_name, Key='foobarbaz')
 259         body = response['Body']
 260         # Am able to set a socket timeout
 261         body.set_socket_timeout(10)
```

I intend to run Formgrader in a Docker container, and then use Jupyterhub to authenticate from there.  I seem to have managed to get the authentication to work, but I'm getting a 404 for everything in /static/, as well as the assignments and students handlers.  

The main difference I can tell between my config on Docker and as a Jupyterhub service is the `base_url` which is set as `'/services/formgrader'` as a Jupyterhub service, and as `'http://127.0.0.1:8000/services/formgrader/'` in Docker, but I haven't figured out where I can change `base_url`.  Is this likely to be causing the problem, or have I missed something because that doesn't seem to quite add up?  Error message is of the form

My config is as follows:

	-p 5000:5000 \
	-e JUPYTERHUB_SERVICE_NAME=formgrader \

issue_title: Simple way to define a model object
issue_body:
If I have a class like:

```
class Model(object):
  def __init__(self):
    self.param1 = 0.
    self.param2 = 0.
  def f(self, x):
    return x * self.param1 + self.param2
```

it would be nice if there was an easy way to declare that Model's parameters are param1 and param2, so that we can then write, inside the class,
```
  g = grad(f)
```
and the gradient would be with respect to param1 and param2.

Or is there a way to do that already?

issue_title: Sharing state between forward and reverse computation
issue_body:
In the [how to define your own primitive](autograd/examples/define_gradient.py) example (logsumexp):

How do I (safely) share state between forward and reverse passes?

I'd like to save ``np.exp(x - max_x)`` from the forward pass, so don't have to compute it again in the backward pass.

One option is to make it an output variable along with logZ. Are there other / preferred options?

The way I normally do this is to have a "logsumexp class" which saves whatever I need from the backward pass in the instance... I'm trying to understand the zen of the functional programming style that autograd uses :-)

Sharing state between forward and backward is even more important in cases like the forward-backward algorithm for CRFs and HMMs, where I want to keep around the whole memo table from the forward pass.

I am trying to use the autograd package with second package that frequently utilizes `float(...)` conversions. However, these operations cause a runtime error: `TypeError: float() argument must be a string or number`.
It is my understanding that autograd assume floating-point numbers anyhow (since differentiating integers does not make much sense); consequently, such a cast should not have any effect on the data type that represents a scalar number in autograd.

Looking at the autograd source, I assume that this data type is an `ArrayNode` with `ndim == 0`, if it represents a scalar.
My first idea was to add a `__float__()` function (see [link](https://docs.python.org/2.0/ref/numeric-types.html)) to `ArrayNode` that supports the casting of scalar values. Something in the line of:
```
def __float__(self):
    if self.ndim > 0:
        raise TypeError('float() argument must be scalar')
    return self
```
However, it seems that a `float()` overload must return a `float` type. Thus, an `ArrayNode` would need to be derived from `float`. Which does not make much sense for multi-dimensional arrays.

My question is thus:
Is there a way to support these superfluous float casts for `ArrayNode`s that are scalar?
https://github.com/galaxyproject/planemo/pull/350
It might be a larger amount of effort than anything else in this project - but it would be cool. See conversation at https://github.com/galaxyproject/tools-devteam/issues/10.

issue_title: reader: incorrect total_rdy update logic when sending new RDY count
issue_body:
In `Reader._send_rdy()` the following block is responsible for updating the reader's `total_rdy` attribute:
```
if conn.send_rdy(value):
      self.total_rdy = max(self.total_rdy - conn.rdy + value, 0)
```
Here `value` represents the desired new `RDY` count; thus if `conn.send_rdy(value)` succeeds we'll have `conn.rdy == value`.  As a result the above will leave `total_rdy` unchanged.   Since there's no other spot in which `total_rdy` is increased, it never gets above its initial value of 0. 

One behavioral consequence of this:  Even if `max_in_flight` is less than the `nsqd` connection count, every connection will get an initial `RDY` count of 1, and so the `max_in_flight` constraint can be violated immediately after a reader begins consuming messages.

For comparison, the go client does this:
```
atomic.AddInt64(&r.totalRdyCount, -c.RDY()+count)
err := c.WriteCommand(Ready(int(count)))
```

issue_body: It appears that the code does not check for the unzip program, which leads to a fairly incomprehensible `No such file or directory` error from mapsplice

The [packaging](https://packaging.pypa.io/en/latest/) library a set of "core utilities for Python packages." One of the things it provides is a set of tools for parsing and comparing versions. The package is provided by the Python Packaging Authority.

The "setuptools" package also provides the ability to parse and compare versions. However, a look at the source code shows that setuptools simply depends on packaging and wraps what packaging provides.

packaging is a more targeted and specific tool. NailGun only uses the versioning capabilities from setuptools, and it seems reasonable for NailGun to make use of a more targeted dependency.

This is a breaking change for the following reasons:

* We're changing dependencies.
* Clients that hook tightly in to NailGun may make use of `pkg_resources.parse_version` and `pkg_resources.SetuptoolsVersion`. (Not sure why, but hey.)

The workflow has a little bit changed, we should document those changes!

When you are working with an APK, you should not use DalvikVMFormat directly as it does not contain all the information. You should always use Analysis object, which contains all VMs and can produce nicer outputs. 
For example, if you need all classes do not use:
```
#d = DalvikVMFormat(...)
d.get_classes()
```
but:
```
#dx = Analysis(...)
#dx.add(DalvikVMFormat(...))
dx.get_classes()
```

DalvikVMFormat should be the basis for Analysis - which contains the raw data. Of course, the pure DalvikVMFormat objects might be of interest as well, but you can still get them and use them.

issue_title: Upgrading dotnet core from 1.0 to 1.1.1 gets stuck on old dependencies in Visual Studio
issue_body:
Hello, when we upgraded from 1.0 to 1.1.1, it failed because all the dependencies in visual studio were still 1.0.  When you clean or rebuild the solution, it still doesn't remove the /obj file.

I was investigating this, and it turned out Visual Studio kept the `obj\project.assets.json` in tact. So any time you rebuild it *reuses* the `project.assets.json` 

This actually made me spend so much time triaging this. Eventually I figured it out. My coworker had this issue as well. Others might have this issue. There are two issues here:

1. The tooling, Visual Studio, should clean "obj" directory after every rebuild/clean.
2. When we Update the "Target framework" in Project properties to `.NETCoreApp 1.1` it should regenerate the `project.assets.json`

I apologize if this isn't the correct place.

The folder should be referenced relatively as each developer may sync the code from source control into a different folder. issue_title: max_attempts in poll_and_ri should be per-authorization
issue_body:
`poll_and_request_issuance([authz1, authz2], max_attempt=1)` is the simplest example, where the code fails, because `max_attempts` counts total number of attempts for all authorizations, instead of counting per authorization. With default limit of 10, this causes problems in practice - usually when people are using more than 10 domains.

Core is not affected, because it doesn't use this retry loop. https://github.com/kuba/simp_le, however, is (and any other client using our library).

```

Error: unauthorized :: The client lacks sufficient authorization :: Error creating new authz :: Syntax error
Please see the logfiles in /var/log/letsencrypt for more details.
```

issue_title: Unable to run the command: apache2ctl configtest
issue_body:
Fresh CentOS 7 Install

yum install httpd (httpd-2.4.6-31.el7.centos.1.x86_64)
yum install mod_ssl (mod_ssl-2.4.6-31.el7.centos.1.x86_64)

./venv/bin/letsencrypt -d subdomain.tld.com auth

Results in this message for option #1:
Apache Web Server - Alpha (apache) [Misconfigured]
More Info:
Reported Error: Unable to run the command: apache2ctl configtest

Run in Standalone Mode obtained certificate

./venv/bin/letsencrypt -d subdomain.tld.com run
"Unable to run the command: apache2ctl configtest"

Looks like the command should be apachectl configtest

issue_title: renewal conf files might not have domains in them
issue_body:
I have one case of this:

```
root@ip-172-31-50-10:/etc/letsencrypt/renewal# grep domains /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/isnot.org.conf 
domains = None
root@ip-172-31-50-10:/etc/letsencrypt/renewal# openssl x509 -inform PEM -in /etc/letsencrypt/live/isnot.org/cert.pem -text -noout | grep -1 Name

            X509v3 Subject Alternative Name: 
                DNS:isnot.org, DNS:is.isnot.org
```

and another case where the file has domains in it.  We need to be consistent about whether we do or don't have domains listed in that file; and if we do, they need to be respected (if not, we should put them in a comment).

issue_title: Asks about HTTPS redirect despite installer not supporting it
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm writing an installer plugin that will not support enhancements, and thus returns none:

    def supported_enhancements(self):
        return []

Nevertheless, on letsencrypt install (with this plugin), I get the choice “Please choose whether HTTPS access is required or optional.”, and then I can choose between Easy or Secure. Shouldn't it check with the installer if it can do the “redirect” enhancement before it asks? It just calls enhance() with no more ifs and buts, which fails.

Currently, the client never modifies a renewal configuration once it's created. Suppose the client obtains a renewal manually, specifying different values on the command line. The renewal config is not updated with these new values.

issue_title: Update Python docs to with correct timestamp granularity
issue_body:
Description
===========
Python documentation incorrectly states that the timestamp field should use microseconds. If we take a look at rdkafka.h however we can see that the expected unit is actually Milliseconds

Python Docs
https://docs.confluent.io/current/clients/confluent-kafka-python/#confluent_kafka.Producer.produce

rdkafka.h
https://github.com/edenhill/librdkafka/blob/13befa7053c43593fd6f2effdffbd83c3ea52eb7/src/rdkafka.h#L804

This will ultimately cause issues from Kafka as the broker will actually record this timestamp which will break things

https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/KAFKA-6939

How to reproduce
================

produce a message with a timestamp with microsecond granularity. 

Checklist
=========
Please provide the following information:

 - [ ] confluent-kafka-python and librdkafka version (`confluent_kafka.version()` and `confluent_kafka.libversion()`):
 - [ ] Apache Kafka broker version:
 - [ ] Client configuration: `{...}`
 - [ ] Operating system:
 - [ ] Provide client logs (with `'debug': '..'` as necessary)
 - [ ] Provide broker log excerpts
 - [ ] Critical issue

But I'd really like to enable richer graphics in various ways.

In Chicago, the MRED MLS needs the full 'RETS-Version' name. So stripping 'RETS/'  in line 118 of session.py throws a version error.  MRED needs 'RETS/1.7.2' or 'RETS/1.5'.  

self.version = str(parser.headers.get('RETS-Version')).strip('RETS/')

MRED requires the following:

self.version = str(parser.headers.get('RETS-Version'))

My bot crashed today with:

```
  File ".../telethon/telegram_client.py", line 226, in invoke
  File ".../telethon/telegram_bare_client.py", line 189, in invoke
  File ".../telethon/network/mtproto_sender.py", line 105, in receive
  File ".../telethon/network/mtproto_sender.py", line 215, in _process_msg
  File ".../telethon/network/mtproto_sender.py", line 299, in _handle_bad_server_salt
  File ".../telethon/network/mtproto_sender.py", line 57, in send
  File ".../telethon/network/mtproto_sender.py", line 57, in send
  File ".../telethon/tl/functions/messages/get_bot_callback_answer.py", line 50, in on_send
  File ".../telethon/extensions/binary_writer.py", line 30, in write_int
OverflowError: int too big to convert
```

This happened in response to:

```
                self.invoke(
                        GetBotCallbackAnswerRequest(
                                peer=get_input_peer(entity),
                                msg_id=message.id,
                                data=data,
                        ))
```

where `message` was a `Message`.

Based on my investigation I think this was caused by the attribute name clash between `MTProtoRequest.msg_id` and `GetBotCallbackAnswerRequest.msg_id`.

I believe what happened was that after my `GetBotCallbackAnswerRequest` was sent for the first time, `MtProtoSender._send_packet` overwrote `request.msg_id` with a freshly generated message id. Then when the request was sent the second time (in the bad salt handler), its `msg_id` was already corrupted. Luckily, it was corrupted with a long integer, which crashed the converter and thus it didn't come thru pointing to a wrong message.

From what I see `msg_id` is also used in some other request classes (such as `GetPaymentFormRequest`) so it should rather be renamed in `MTProtoRequest`.

issue_title: Method fields with allow_none=False should be absent from serialization
issue_body:
I'm working on a json schema serializer and an issue that I keep running into is that a method field returning 'None' serializes to 'null' even with allow_none set to false.

```Python
class TestSchema(Schema):
    test_field = fields.Method('test_method', allow_none=False)

    def test_method(self, obj):
        pass

> s = TestSchema()
> s.dump('')
# Result currently returned
MarshalResult(data={u'test_field': None}, errors={})
# Result expected
MarshalResult(data={}, errors={})
```

Currently I am getting around the issue by including 'delattr(self, 'field')' in the method, but it feels a bit hacky.

I've recently experimented with 'only' to be used as GET query parameter for REST API and went as far as making a PR where exclude's set difference is still applied to only in '_update_fields'.

However, this turned out to bring even more issues regarding attaching REST with marshmallow. If I specify non-existing attribute to only, schema.dump will raise AttributeError. The common python sense would dictate that I should catch the error here and return 404 response for including non-existing attribute.

The problem is if excluded attribute is requested as fields, it will not throw any error and will just exclude those attributes from the response, resulting in an inconsistency and possibly vulnerability in API.

That's why I was thinking maybe fields option should define the boundary of the only using set intersection. This sort of makes exclude redundant, though.

Any thoughts?

model = session.query(Model).get(1)
TestSchema().dump(model).data

    {u'position': None}

issue_title: inplace option in layers needs refactory
issue_body:
There're several problems with inplace option in layers:

- some layers has inplace option while others not. 
- within the layers that has inplace option, some modifies input and change it back in backward (e.g. AddConstant), while some just modify the input wihthout change it back (e.g. Dropout). 
- layer's input changed in place may cause unwanted results if used carelessly
     - if inplace change is used as the first layer, user's direct input will be changed. 
     - in branches, if the last layer's output is changed by the first branch, the second branch may get wrong input.

should this page not be added to the docs itself?

https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/wiki/Projects-and-publications-that-use-pvlib-python

I do not use `tracking` but working on PR330 it seems to me that there is something wrong.

https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/blob/master/pvlib/tracking.py#L113

```python
try:
   solar_zenith = kwargs['solar_zenith']
except KeyError:
    solar_zenith = self.solar_zenith
```

First of all one can simply write:  `solar_zenith = kwargs.get('solar_zenith', self.solar_zenith')`.

But at the moment the `solar_zenith` is neither an attribute of the class nor of the parent class. Furthermore the `solar_zenith` (`apparent_zenith`) is not obligate, but I should be. So we should change it to something like this:

```diff
- def get_irradiance(self, dni, ghi, dhi,
-                    dni_extra=None, airmass=None, model='haydavies',
-                    **kwargs):
+ def get_irradiance(self, dni, ghi, dhi, solar_zenith, solar_azimuth,
+                    dni_extra=None, airmass=None, model='haydavies',
+                    **kwargs):
-    try:
-        solar_zenith = kwargs['solar_zenith']
-    except KeyError:
-        solar_zenith = self.solar_zenith
```

To me it seems to be some kind of relict because according to the docstrings the `zenith` parameter is obligate.

You will find the same issue for the `azimuth` angle.

I think it is not urgent, because most users will understand that they have to pass `zenith` and `azimuth` to get the total irradiation and I will not have the time to write the according tests. 

Maybe somebody who works with tracking may want to bring that in line.

When setting up a new application you have to do the following steps:

1. `senza init` guides you through an interactive process which creates several *base* resources for all the following application stacks. Those may include security groups, IAM roles, etc. Additionally a senza file is created which allows you to use...
2. `senza create` to create stack versions.
3. `senza delete` to delete a stack version.

Why not have `senza init` create an actual CloudFormation stack which includes the resources created? Let's call this stack **application-id-base.yaml**. The generated **application-id.yaml** references resources from this base stack (security groups, IAM roles, ...).

1. After generating the **application-id-base.yaml** `senza init` allows the user the modify her base stack (e.g. adapting the security group configurations) - the base stack is created afterwards
2. Having the **application-id-base.yaml** allows you the easily deploy a application as-is somewhere else (see https://github.com/zalando-stups/senza/issues/98) and it allows to delete an application completely (see https://github.com/zalando-stups/senza/issues/97)

What do you think? What did I miss? :grin:
As soon as https://github.com/zalando-stups/pierone/issues/33 is done.

_From [ra666...@googlemail.com](https://code.google.com/u/105054707325265245390/) on March 03, 2014 15:42:47_
    
    Hello,
    
    after encoding an AAC (m4a) file e.g. with the FDK AAC encoder the 'meta' atom tree is written to the file including the '\xa9too' (encoded by) tag.
    
    After re-editing the meta atom structure with mutagen.MP4 a 'free' padding atom is written unnecessarily expanding the file size (up to 1024 bytes?) For users not having a focus on the tag writing speed benefits it's wasting of the storage space. Is there any other reasons for that?
    
    In addition there are some AAC player implementations which obviously have issues with 'free' atoms within the meta atom tree even if it's iTunes format conform.
    
    Therefore, an implementation of a function which re-writes the atom tree structure from scratch without any padding would by highly appreciated.
    
    To my understanding MP4.delete() does not solve the issue.
    
    Thank you!
    
    ra666ack

issue_title: ExprRef's __eq__  returns either Boolean or BoolRef
issue_body:
Z3py's ExprRef is equipped with  \_\_hash\_\_, which should allow us to  use ExprRef  as a dictionary key; ExprRef also  implements \_\_eq\_\_, but apparently it returns either Boolean or BoolRef.  

So I am somewhat concerned about whether it is right and safe to use ExprRef in a hashtable ? Maybe something I misunderstand?

 follows:
   
    def __eq__(self, other):
        """Return a Z3 expression that represents the constraint `self == other`.

        If `other` is `None`, then this method simply returns `False`.

        >>> a = Int('a')
        >>> b = Int('b')
        >>> a == b
        a == b
        >>> a == None
        False
        """
        if other == None:
            return False
        a, b = _coerce_exprs(self, other)
        return BoolRef(Z3_mk_eq(self.ctx_ref(), a.as_ast(), b.as_ast()), self.ctx)

Hi!
I just played a little with the FP support in the unstable branch (in Python), and while doing so, I noticed some things that I think could be improved:
- the `fpTo*` functions that convert FP values to something else return `FPRef`s instead of what they convert to. This is a problem because some functions and operators don't work correctly this way if for example both of their arguments are `fpToIEEEBV(foo)`
- there are `fpTo*` functions that actually convert from something to FP (like `fpToFPUnsigned`) and the naming makes this pretty confusing (something like `UBVToFp` would be much clearer, mirroring `fpToUBV`)
- this is mostly the expansion of the previous item; it would be nice if all the conversion functions would be consistently named.

That is all for now, If I notice anything else I'll add it. If I'm wrong about something, please do tell me :)

issue_title: Logging class has some global/instance confusion
issue_body:
1. In the Flask world, `init_app()` is used used for manager objects that are Globally instantiated but work in the context of multiple flask apps.  Since the Logging instance doesn't really do that, each Logging instance is dedicated to a single app instance, all it's init could happen in `__init__()`.
2. But, each Logging instance is operating on the Global root logger by default, with some help from `clear_keg_handlers()` to make sure we don't create duplicate handlers.  In essence, this is creating a last-app-loaded-wins situation, which may not be desirable.

issue_title: the validator should be able to be called as a library method
issue_body:
The elevator "calls" the stix2 validator as sort of a library.  The elevator should just use the validator via its API (command line args).  Currently, the elevator is parsing the validator's args and setting some of them directly in ValidatorOptions - which should be hidden.

The main method should be rewritten, so you just pass in the command line arg string.  Then the new main method of the validator is just a wrapper around that library method.

Would be nice to support cyclic pooling and cyclic rolling in Lasagne out-of-the-box. It has been shown useful for many tasks (Galaxy classification, Plankton classification, and probably also for playing Go).

References:
* http://benanne.github.io/2015/03/17/plankton.html#architecture
* https://github.com/benanne/kaggle-ndsb/blob/master/dihedral_fast.py

Hi, perhaps this is already widely known, but it recently occurred to me that nonlinearities are a somewhat hackish way to introduce arbitrary Theano expressions into your network graph.  In particular, the NonlinearityLayer can be used to compute any sort of theano expression you want between two layers; e.g. in this questionable use-case:

```Python
l_in = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((2, 3))
l_print = lasagne.layers.NonlinearityLayer(
    l_in, nonlinearity=lambda x: theano.printing.Print('x: ')(x))
out = lasagne.layers.get_output(l_print)
compute_out = theano.function([l_in.input_var], out)
compute_out(np.array([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]], dtype=theano.config.floatX))
```

will result in this being printed out:

```
x:  __str__ = [[ 1.  2.  3.]
 [ 4.  5.  6.]]
```

This is true for any layer with a nonlinearity; here we are printing and using a Tanh nonlinearity both:

```Python
l_in = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((2, 3))
l_out = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(
    l_in, 4, nonlinearity=lambda x: theano.printing.Print('x: ')(T.tanh(x)))
out = lasagne.layers.get_output(l_out)
compute_out = theano.function([l_in.input_var], out)
compute_out(np.array([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]], dtype=theano.config.floatX))
```

If this is something we'd like to encourage, I'd suggest putting it in an FAQ list #298 somewhere under "How can I include arbitrary calculations in Lasagne networks?" and maybe even making an alias for NonlinearityLayer called ExpressionLayer (or something) with self.nonlinearity renamed self.expression.  Note that you can also do this in a maybe less hacky but a bit more cumbersome way by calling `lasagne.layers.get_output`, modifying the resulting Theano expression, and using it as the input variable to a new `InputLayer` that then feeds the rest of your network.

It seems like semantic segmentation is becoming a popular task, so there are quite a few questions on the mailing list lately about doing per-pixel classification. This currently requires some trickery because Theano's softmax function expects 2D input, so you have to shuffle and reshape your 'output maps' for this to work (latest discussion: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/lasagne-users/6P1bodBbhWs )

Perhaps we should add a helper function or layer `SpatialSoftmax`, that does this sequence of operations? Or more generally we could have `Softmax1D`, `Softmax2D` etc.?

One thing we have to be careful about is that it is not confused with a softmax across spatial locations (rather than a per-pixel softmax operation), which is sometimes used for attention mechanisms. Perhaps we should provide a helper for that use case as well.

issue_title: Ignoring default currency, creating new records with XYZ, latest package 0.3.4 broken
issue_body:
Hello.

Have multiple issues. Firstly upgraded to 0.3.3.2 package. I have defined default currency:

    price_list = MoneyField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2, default_currency='EUR')

And also in settings.py:

    CURRENCIES = ('EUR', )

Despite of this, all new records in db are created with currency XYZ. 


Then I found out that new 0.3.4 package is online, I hoped that will fix these issues and package tarball is empty, no setup.py and sources:

![screenshot from 2013-11-26 18 16 59](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/115118/1623650/60b4518e-56b6-11e3-8ceb-e91b190ffd3d.png)

Now we're have some problems, which are related to using of `moneyed.Money`.

### F expressions support is not complete

This is possible:

```python
F('field') + Money(1, 'EUR')
```

But this one will rise ``TypeError``:

```python
Money(1, 'EUR') + F('field')
```

```TypeError: Cannot add or subtract a Money and non-Money instance.```

The ``moneyed`` package should not know about Django's ``F`` expressions, and we need to implement it on the `django-money` side to fix the issue.

### Lacking of clarity about MoneyPatched

IMO the name doesn't clarify the purpose of the class. Sometimes we're using it, sometimes not, but it is not clear why.

### What to do?

Use only `Money` from `djmoney`, not from `moneyed`, which will be a subclass of ``moneyed.Money`` and will contain all additional logic to support Django. 
If we will use this class internally and encourage users to use it, than we will need only one entity instead of two. In this case we can guarantee, that all Django features will be supported by this class, we can't do it for ``moneyed.Money``.
Probably, we should support ``moneyed.Money`` as we do now, but add a note, that in this case some features are not supported.

So, my proposal is:

- Rename ``MoneyPatched`` -> ``Money``
- Add complete ``F`` expressions support
- Replace all usages of ``moneyed.Money`` to ``djmoney.Money``
- Update documentation accordingly

@benjaoming , what do you think?

issue_title: WagtailTranslationOptions doesn't find all Page models
issue_body:
In 0.6rc1, the new `WagtailTranslationOptions` applies the standard base fields to any model that derives from `Page`. However, I noticed a model that had multiple inheritances (specifically a form based on `WagtailCaptchaEmailForm` which is based on `AbstractEmailForm` which is based on `AbstractForm` which is based on `Page`), wasn't picked up by if options.

On Python 2.7, Django 1.8.13, `model.__bases__` only returns the direct parent of the Page model. I added `or Page in model._meta.get_parent_list()` which solved my problem.

So the entire if statement in WagtailTranslationOptions could read:
` if Page in model.__bases__ or Page in model._meta.get_parent_list():`

Or, perhaps there's a better way to get the base class of a model?

Example:
```
push eax
push ebx
call some_func
test eax, eax
jne 0xxxxx
```

Can angr skip the `some_func` function?

issue_title: How to skip an instruction during symbolic execution?
issue_body:
I use angr to do symbolic execution. the arch is arm/thumb.
The basck block's assembler code are below:
```
.text:000016CA               MULS          R3, R2
.text:000016CC               SUBS           R4, R4, R3
.text:000016CE               BL               some_func
.text:000016D2               MOV            R3, 3
```
My purpose is skip the instruction in 0x16CE，That is not to execute the instruction like BL/BLX/BX。

The ways I tried are:
1. Use ```inspect.b(0x4102FD, hook_change, length=0)```, then change the PC register in the ```hook_change()```. 0x4102FD is the address of some_func.
2. Change the PC register or ```state.scratch.ins_addr``` directly.

My idea is to modify the PC register or to change the ins address of the current state.
But it did't work.
I'm not very familiar with angr, who can tell me how to do?

Hi Guys!

I've been using gymrat lifter recently and came into exception:

```
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/angr/analyses/cfg/cfg_fast.py", line 3376, in _generate_cfgnode
    irsb = lifted_block.vex
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/angr/block.py", line 145, in vex
    arch=self.arch,
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/angr/engines/vex/engine.py", line 472, in lift
    opt_level=opt_level)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyvex/block.py", line 79, in __init__
    lift(self, arch, mem_addr, data, max_bytes, max_inst, bytes_offset, opt_level, traceflags)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyvex/lift/__init__.py", line 155, in lift
    next_irsb_part = lifter(arch, addr)._lift(u_data, bytes_offset, max_bytes, max_inst, opt_level, traceflags, allow_lookback)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyvex/lift/__init__.py", line 67, in _lift
    self.lift()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyvex/lift/util/lifter_helper.py", line 82, in lift
    instructions = self.decode()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyvex/lift/util/lifter_helper.py", line 77, in decode
    raise e
bitstring.ReadError: Reading off the end of the data. Tried to read 16 bits when only 8 available.
```

The reason for this is following:

When shared library is built for an ARM, if no entry point is specified for it, it will be set to the begining of the "_.text_" section. And address will be always as for the ARM mode, with last bit of address equal to 0.

However, that library can have exported function compiled for thumb mode which is right at the begining of "_.text_" section

For example EP is at **0xbd688** and exported_func is at **0xbd689** according to symbols.

When CFGFast._generate_cfgnode(...) is called for EP address (**0xbd688**) "distance heuristics" inside this function will cut size to 1 byte (**0xbd689**-**0xbd688**=1):

https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/8d60db6de8ebc987b3d19650f55ec263765559ad/angr/analyses/cfg/cfg_fast.py#L3324-L3333

Obviously there is no 1 byte instructions in ARM, so it is marked as undecoded and GymratLifter is called.

GymratLifter gets 1 byte stream, and when my two byte instruction is parsed from stream, it fails here:

https://github.com/angr/pyvex/blob/9e4c3e12d52c61bbaa1f22f731dbb86b9e5e6cef/pyvex/lift/util/instr_helper.py#L157-L165

As a workaround I exclude entry point from analysis if there is symbol at address EP+1.

I thought may be we could add some code to  CFGFast.\_\_init\_\_() to solve this problem?

For example, change this

https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/8d60db6de8ebc987b3d19650f55ec263765559ad/angr/analyses/cfg/cfg_fast.py#L741

To

```
if self.project.loader.main_object.symbols_by_addr[self.project.entry+1] and \
        self.project.arch.name in ('ARMHF', 'ARMEL'):
    self._start_at_entry = False

issue_title: overriding settings from the command line should only affect the current invocation
issue_body:
The `mpsyt set... , set...` feature is very helpful, but it seems to change mpsyt settings permanently, and this can lead to unexpected results in subsequent invocations of the program.

Scenario: as I do for many UNIX programs, I wrote a shell script wrapper for mpsyt, for a very specific need which requires overriding the standard settings via command line parameters. Currently, if I run mpsyt directly, after running my wrapper, mpsyt will keep using the special settings, instead of the expected standard settings.

The expected behaviour, like for standard UNIX programs, is that overriding settings by command-line options only affects the current instance of the program, without permanently changing settings in the program configuration file.

### Steps to reproduce

install !

setup.py", line 12, in <module>
        from pip.req import parse_requirements

python 3.5

### solution
Seems to be an incompatibility with new version of pip (>=10)
https://stackoverflow.com/a/49867265/1619521

so have to change 
`from pip.req import parse_requirements`
to
```
try: # for pip >= 10

except ImportError: # for pip <= 9.0.3
    from pip.req import parse_requirements
```

Allowing something like cass.get_match_history(summoner_id=123, region=123) would significantly speed up tasks.

These cass objects currently do allow you to pass either a cass.Summoner, str, or int obj but the initialization asserts for cass.Summoner.

issue_title: Calc_loc for specifying previous OptimizeFW for a StaticFW?
issue_body:
I'm cleaning up some old MatMethods workflows to push to atomate and I noticed that the current atomate.vasp.fireworks.StaticFW no longer has keywords for calc_loc.

Within a single workflow, I run OptimizeFW for two structures in parallel followed by a StaticFW for each structure (these structures are then used together later to create interpolated structures). I use calc_loc to specify which of the OptimizeFW runs the StaticFW should use for copy_vasp_outputs.

It's my understanding that calc_loc = True will grab the location of the most recently completed calculation (rather than the parent) so I cannot use it for this situation. Is this the case? If so, what's the best work around? Creating a new class that inherits from StaticFW that has this parameter?

issue_title: matminer can include capability to functionalize features
issue_body:
Part 1: This would be a tool that takes in a feature (or list of features) and returns some functional outputs like:

* x
* 1/x
* x^05.
* x^-0.5
* x^2
* x^-2
* x^3
* x^-3
* ln(x)
* 1/ln(x)
* exp(x)
* exp(-x)

where x is the original feature value. Note that the list of features above is from "Machine Learning and Materials Informatics: Recent Applications and Prospects" by Ramprasad et al.

Part 2: This would take in a list of features (either raw features or perhaps after applying the functions above) and combine them, i.e., give you x1*x2 for all features. Note that if you applied the functions above, you will also have features like x1, x2, 1/x1, 1/x2, etc... so by multiplying all feature combinations you will end up with things like x1*x2, x1/x2, x2/x1, etc. Unfortunately, some of these will just be 1 (x1*1/x1) or redundant (x1*x1^2 is the same as x1^3).

issue_title: Verify that duplicate userid is not allowed
issue_body:
https://pulp.plan.io/issues/2957
Assert user create does not allow creation of duplicate userid.

Hello,

In DDPG, when an episode reaches the maximum amount of steps of an environment, the time_limit wrapper returns done=True even if the state reached is not terminal. Then when doing target q values computation, this same value of terminal=True is used, leading to the target being only the reward for this particular "false" terminal state, without bootstrapping. As pointed out here http://arxiv.org/abs/1712.00378, this leads to incoherent updates.

This bug may be found in other algos but i did not check. If I have missed anything, please let me know !

issue_title: conversion scripts should accept long or short file paths
issue_body:
Conversion scripts like sio_magic should be able to take long or short file names.  I.e.:

    sio_magic.py -f infile.txt -WD path/to/

or

    sio_magic.py -f path/to/infile.txt

or, of course, if you're in the path/to directory:

    sio_magic.py -f infile.txt

To support this, I've added a pmag function resolve_file_name that takes the filename and the working directory (if available).  It prioritizes the long file name, then if that doesn't work tries to create a long file name with working directory + the filename, and if that fails create a long file name with "." + the filename.  

Once implemented, this should be more flexible _and_ less confusing.

issue_title: Binary classification just learns the major class

I am using TFlearn to train a case about binary classifiction, expected output is 0 or 1.
And the training Input is images' pixels corresponding to labels 0 or 1, but the major labels are 0.
For example, Input is 60*80 = 4800 pixels and the number of label 0 has 3800, the number of labe 1 has 1000. In this situation, I start to training.

First iteration, the accuracy is about 0.73
...
Until 200 iteration when it almost converges, the accuracy is about 0.79

And I use model.predict to predict the model, I found the model only learn the label 0 class, the label 1 class just be considered the wrong case. When the iteration increases, the label 0 class learn better, but the label 1 class can't learn at all.

For example, Using model.predict to predict 0 class gets [[0.8630272746086121, 0.02531861700117588]]
                     Using model.predict to predict 1 class gets [[0.8631386756896973,0.025179509073495865]]

Right now the help command just gives you an alphabetical columnar list of commands. It would be nice if there was a way to create groups or categories of commands, so you could have related commands show up together.

Instead of this:

```
Documented commands (type help <topic>):
========================================
config     expire       load      resources   shell                stop
connect    findleakers  py        restart     shortcuts            threaddump
deploy     help         pyscript  serverinfo  show                 undeploy
edit       history      quit      sessions    sslconnectorciphers  version
exit       license      redeploy  set         start                vminfo
exit_code  list         reload    settings    status               which
```
It would be nice if you could create something like:
```
Documented commands (for more info type help <command>)

Connecting
=========================================
connect     which

Application Management
=========================================
list
deploy       redeploy      undeploy
start        restart       stop
sessions     expire        findleakers

Server Information
==========================================

status            thread dump     vminfo

Settings
==========================================

Other
=============================
edit      help        license
exit      history     load
version
```

It would be great if you could not only control the sections of the help, but the order and formatting of the commands within each section. Commands not specified for inclusion in a specific section would be featured in a catch all section like **Other**.

It might also be interesting to have a way to specify a short phrase of help for each command, with some command like **help --long** to show:

```
Documented commands (for more info type help <command>)

Connecting
=========================================
connect    connect to a tomcat server
which      show which server you are currently connected to
...
```

issue_title: set debug true warning in perror
issue_body:
Hi!

In [this issue](https://github.com/uchicago-cs/chistributed/issues/11) from the `chistributed` framework, which implements its repl using `cmd2`, we encountered an issue where `set` was defined as a command, making the `set debug true` warning confusing. I noticed that this is printed conditionally based on a parameter to `perror`, and no option to control this is exposed to the end user.

It seems to me that either `set` should be a privileged command that can't be overloaded, or the end user should be given this option. It could be passed in at initialization, and used if defined (falling back to the current values).

Any thoughts? I'm happy to submit a patch. 🎈

issue_title: Hang when calling method from extended C++ class in future
Why is linking to Python debug library disabled for Windows?
issue_body:
From detail/common.h

```
/// Include Python header, disable linking to pythonX_d.lib on Windows in debug mode
#if defined(_MSC_VER)
#  if (PY_MAJOR_VERSION == 3 && PY_MINOR_VERSION < 4)
#    define HAVE_ROUND 1
#  endif
#  pragma warning(push)
#  pragma warning(disable: 4510 4610 4512 4005)
#  if defined(_DEBUG)
#    define PYBIND11_DEBUG_MARKER
#    undef _DEBUG
#  endif
#endif
```
Since I need debug build this undef _DEBUG caused me a lot of grief until I dicovered it.

On top of wanting to be able to debug my own code, I don't mind being able to step through Python for Windows dll code as well. In fact, in a few cases it was very useful.

Coul anyone please explain why would one want to undef _DEBUG?

issue_title: Replace lineage whitelist with a blacklist
issue_body:
Is it worse for renewal conf files to be cluttered or for important values to be forgotten? I think the latter is worse.

This will prevent bugs like #4105, however, we probably want to properly solve #893 first otherwise we may be unable to restore some config items. (Alternatively, we could just drop items we can't properly restore, but I think solving #893 is a better approach.)

issue_title: Allow clients to specify a device to open using a set of unique device information.
issue_body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Currently clients pass a list index to ``core.Harvester.get_image_acquisition_agent`` method to specify a device to open. However, there might be a case where the client knows a set of information to specify a single device. Harvester Core should support a way to accept such a set of information.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
Add parameters to ``core.Harvester.get_image_acquisition_agent`` so that a client can list up a set of information to specify a single device to open. At least the following information must be supported:

* ``unique_id``: ``DEVICE_INFO_ID``
* ``vendor``: ``DEVICE_INFO_VENDOR``
* ``model``: ``DEVICE_INFO_MODEL``
* ``tl_type``: ``DEVICE_INFO_TLTYPE``
* ``user_defined_name``: ``DEVICE_INFO_USER_DEFINED_NAME``
* ``serial_number``: ``DEVICE_INFO_SERIAL_NUMBER``
* ``version``: ``DEVICE_INFO_VERSION``

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
None.

**Additional context**
None.

Use a declarative architecture similar to elm/redux to facilitate Raiden testing and recoverability (#205).

#### Requirements:

- Use a stateless / referential transparent state machine.
  - This guarantees that all the state required to restart the node will be contained outside of the state machine.
  - The state machine is just a function that receives the current state and a new state transition to be applied, the result is a **new** state.
- Use a declarative approach based for state transitions of the state machine.
  - This allows for state transitions to be journaled in a [WAL](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Write-ahead_logging).
  - Protocol messages and blockchain events should be encoded as events.
- The resulting actions from the state machine allows the node to communicate with the external world.
  - Messages sent through the network or events used to notify the node user.
  - The state machine **cannot** produce actions that result in new state transitions, this remove the possibility of infinite loops on the handling of events.

#### Implemented in a PR

The following are currently implemented in the work in progress PR: https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/266

- The base architecture and markers
- The mediated transfer as a state machine.
  - The initiator role
  - The mediator role
  - The target role
  - Declared the valid protocol messages for each role as a state transition.
  - Declared the blockchain events relevant for each role as a state transition.

#### Not yet implemented

- The result events that can trigger events from the Raiden node or a client connected to it.

issue_title: Implement a queue of pending transactions
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

A queue of pending IO operations is required in order to make every state snapshotable, currently there is such a queue for [unconfirmed messages](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/transfer/state.py#L88), but there are not queue for pending transactions. This queue is required in order to safely implement snapshots.

#### TODOs

- [x] Add a queue for pending transactions
- [ ] Add state changes to confirm transactions, these state changes must be emitted after the polling confirmed it and it's side effect is to remove the corresponding transaction from the queue.

## Problem Definition

Opening and closing channels would mean that the UI will show all channels the user created. This issue is to suggest we add some UI utility which would enable me to toggle (show/hide) settled channels.

issue_title: Create a raiden module and use remapping for smart contracts
issue_body:
To have the smart contracts easily useable by solidity and by other modules in the future we should use solc "as it was intended" and utilize [remappings](http://solidity.readthedocs.io/en/latest/layout-of-source-files.html?highlight=import#use-in-actual-compilers).

Essentially at compile time we should add a remapping like:
`solc --optimize raiden=/home/lefteris/ew/raiden/raiden/smart_contracts`.

And then all smart contracts should use `raiden` as their prefix. For example

```
import "raiden/NettingChannelLibrary.sol"
```

issue_title: Django Error when using multiple fixtures and factory.Iterator
issue_body:
We have several models with `ForeignKey`.

These models have factories using `factory.Iterator` on the same tables (Ex: `Countries.objects.all()`, `Currencies.objects.all()`, etc.)
Ex:  ```CurrencyID = factory.Iterator(models.Currencies.objects.all()) ```

We have multiple django test cases using different fixtures with different data for these tables (Ex: A fixture with the country "Afghanistan", another fixture with "Canada" and "USA").

With SQLite it gives this kind of error:

```<AppName>.models.DoesNotExist: Countries matching query does not exist.```

With MySQL it gives this kind of error:
```django.db.utils.IntegrityError: (1452, 'Cannot add or update a child row: a foreign key constraint fails (`test_<AppName>`.`SomeModel`, CONSTRAINT `SomeModel_CurrencyID_2734797b_fk_Currencies_id` FOREIGN KEY (`CurrencyID`) REFERENCES `Currencies` (`id`))')```

Tested with `factory-boy` 2.7.0 and 2.8.1 with `django` 1.9.

When many tests are ran the bug can occur. When running the tests individually they succeed.

I assume there is a problem with the iterator when unloading and loading fixtures on these table. The iterator may not be reinitialized between tests cases.

Workarounds:
1. Use the same data for these tables (either same fixture or same data in different fixtures)
2. Replace the iterator with a lamda that will randomly select a row in the table
3. Replace the iterator with a subfactory (but does not work well with some models)

issue_title: BUG: data gets saved to database twice
issue_body:
### Steps to reproduce

I ran a 2D scan using the do2d function in my pull request, which is basically a copy/paste of the do2d function provided in the qcodes examples for the new dataset.

`do2d(qdac.lp, -0.75, -0.9, 500, 0.07, qdac.b, -0.5, 0.5, 100, 0.07, lockin_2.X, lockin_2.Y)`

For completeness I provide the function here:

```
def do2d(param_set1, start1, stop1, num_points1, delay1,
         param_set2, start2, stop2, num_points2, delay2,
         *param_meas):
    '''Scan 2D of param_set and measure param_meas.'''
    meas = Measurement()
    refresh_time = 1. # in s
    meas.write_period = refresh_time
    meas.register_parameter(param_set1)
    param_set1.post_delay = delay1
    meas.register_parameter(param_set2)
    param_set2.post_delay = delay2
    output = []
    for parameter in param_meas:
        meas.register_parameter(parameter, setpoints=(param_set1, param_set2))
        output.append([parameter, None])
    progress_bar = progressbar.ProgressBar(max_value=num_points1 * num_points2)
    points_taken = 0
    time.sleep(0.1)
    
    with meas.run() as datasaver:
        run_id = datasaver.run_id
        last_time = time.time()
        for set_point1 in np.linspace(start1, stop1, num_points1):
            param_set1.set(set_point1)
            for set_point2 in np.linspace(start2, stop2, num_points2):
                param_set2.set(set_point2)
                for i, parameter in enumerate(param_meas):
                    output[i][1] = parameter.get()
                datasaver.add_result((param_set1, set_point1),
                                     (param_set2, set_point2),
                                     *output)
                points_taken += 1
                current_time = time.time()
                if current_time - last_time >= refresh_time:
                    last_time = current_time
                    progress_bar.update(points_taken)
        progress_bar.update(points_taken)
    return run_id
```

### Expected behavior

Each point should get saved once.

### Actual behaviour

Some points get saved more than once (which destroys alignment of the data if your data analysis relies on it). Excerpt from the table:

issue_title: AttributeError: ‘list’ object has no attribute ‘data’ in pascal notebook

issue_body:
Hello,

If I worked and submitted a PR for incorporating observational weights (i.e. each observation can have a potentially different influence on the loss function) would it have a chance of being approved? Observational weights can be important for structured data whereby a certain record has been observed for a long period time than another record and therefore carries more information. This is very common when working with insurance data for instance where any one record can represent a varying length of an insurance contract.

I think I know how to incorporate observational weights without too much detritus but it will require some extra conditionals here and there in `fit()` and `Stepper`. It will also require users to write custom pytorch loss functions and evaluation metric functions since pytorch doesn't support observational weights.

Let me know and I'd be happy to make a pass at this.

Thanks,
Patrick

issue_title: Any tricks to lazily load dataset when creating Iterator?
issue_body:
Currently OpenNMT-py encounters a memory hogging problem when using large corpus(size of several MBs), when totally loaded in to create `torchtext.data.Dataset`, it will occupy ~GBs memory.

Our (proposed) approach is to divide the large corpus into small ones, and create `torchtext.data.Dataset` for each separately when preprocessing. This approach works fine.

But when do training, this problem emerges again. Because we need to create `torchtext.data.Iterator`, which takes `dataset` as argument. Two possible approaches:

1)  Coalesce multiple datasets into one and pass to `torchtext.data.Iterator`. This approach requires all
    datasets loaded at the same time, so the memory hogging problem comes back.
2)  Create  each `torchtext.data.Iterator` for each `dataset`, and make an iterator chain to iterate over all data.  This actually also requires dataset to be loaded when the Iterator is created, unless we postpone the creation of `Iterator`. And it is hard to iterate across the dataset boundary, because our current `__iter__` yields a wrapper `Batch`.

Any tricks to tackle this problem?  I figure we could add support for accepting multiple datasets and do lazy load when create Iterator.  Thought?  Thanks.

issue_title: Actor creation warning can fire spuriously under heavy load.
issue_body:
On a cluster with 98 nodes (1 m4.16xlarge and 97 m4.xlarge), there are a total of 452 CPUs. Doing the following

```python
@ray.remote(num_cpus=1)
class Foo(object):
    pass

fs = [Foo.remote() for _ in range(452)]
```

leads to a number of warning messages of the form

*The actor with ID 0ac1331a6bab10c0b239cf1ebdf5f92a976de0e7 is taking a while to be created. It is possible that the cluster does not have enough resources to place this actor. Try reducing the number of actors created or increasing the number of slots available by using the --num-cpus, --num-gpus, and --resources flags.*

Right now a lot of spillback is required to place these actors properly, which triggers this warning. Note that the likelihood of this error can be reduced dramatically with more intelligent scheduling.
Tests aren't running anymore with Python2.6, we can probably just drop support.

* crispy 1.6.0 * django 1.8 * python 3.4 This is more of a question for than an issue. I've read the doc and i followed the procedure for an ajax validation. ``` form = UserForm(request.POST or None) if form.is_valid(): form.instance.user = request.user form.save() # do something ctx = {} ctx.update(csrf(request)) form_html = render_crispy_form(form, helper=form.helper,context=ctx) #return new form ``` In the ajax success, the doc says that we have to replace the old form with the new one. ex: $(example_form).replaceWith(data['form_html']); this works fine but what happens if instead of putting the form in the template like so: {% crispy form %} I render the form with each field like so
{{ form.first_name|as_crispy_field }} {{ form.first_name.errors }}
if i replace the old form with the new one, I would lose any custom css that I set on my form. Is there a way to replace each field one by one instead of the whole form? Thanks!

There are at least 3 separate issues with this output.

(a)
Vulnerability #1 should not be in the output, or at least, if you would argue it should be, you'd concede it's a good idea to give an option for vulnerabilities like it to not be in the output. When I say 'vulnerabilities like it' I mean, we ran it on a controller file, `upload_controller.py` which calls into `misc.py`, then we reported vulnerabilities as though we ran it on `misc.py`, resulting in a duplicate (vulnerabilities 1 and 2). 

To solve this, maybe we should do something with `self.filenames[-1]` inside of `interprocedural.py` or just, at a higher level, grab the file from the -f output and skip any vulnerabilities that don't match it (note the `File: example/django.nV/taskManager/misc.py` in the output). The latter idea sounds cleaner and smoother.

(b) Vulnerability #3 is not unknown, although we know `uploaded_file` is tainted we don't have any idea if `uploaded_file.temporary_file_path()` is something that leads to a vulnerability.

To solve this, we somehow add the return value of `uploaded_file.temporary_file_path()` to blackbox_assignments. The .args list of the sink might include `uploaded_file`, so we'll need to change this as well.

(c) 

A 4th idea that I noticed, but I'm not going to implement, is that a good heuristic for what a Django controller is is that it has request as the first argument. This is easy enough to implement in an hour by looking at https://github.com/python-security/pyt/pull/52/files though, if you're interested.

issue_title: Print out the bgen file specification version

issue_body:
The `to_address()` method on the `P2pkhScript` returns a p2sh address, while the `address()` method returns the expected p2pkh address. This happens because the class itself does not implement the `to_address()` and only inherits it from `ScriptPubKey` where it is hardcoded to always give a p2sh if it's not segwit. I can see two ways to fix this, either in the parent or the child class:
In the parent class change the `address()` method to default to p2sh and change the `else` part in to_address to `return self.address()`. This preserves the old behaviour for child classes that do not implement `address()` but fixes it for those that do.
Or change the child class(es) to implement `to_address()`.

Edit: created a PR with a proposed solution (only touches parent class)

Below a minimal test case that shows the unexpected/surprising behaviour:

```import btcpy

from btcpy.structs.script import P2pkhScript
from btcpy.structs.crypto import PublicKey

btcpy.setup.setup('mainnet')
script = P2pkhScript(PublicKey.unhexlify('02f1a8ff45acaf2c4405436e44302fcf5e56c1c366e5ad2aebb148e8de4aa92341'))
print(script.to_address()) # 39GzTi9aMuVUwPX9DxxokygtLNvfBLzPhR
print(script.address()) # 1HXWXZovrY7rT7TFQq2dmd9knPFYAXa6VG
assert script.to_address() == script.address()
```

I've been experimenting with the flags module and had a question. From the documentation:

> """This package is used to define and parse command line flags.
This package defines a *distributed* flag-definition policy: rather than
an application having to define all flags in or near main(), each Python
module defines flags that are useful to it.  When one Python module
imports another, it gains access to the other's flags.  (This is
implemented by having all modules share a common, global registry object
containing all the flag information.)
Flags are defined through the use of one of the DEFINE_xxx functions.
The specific function used determines how the flag is parsed, checked,
and optionally type-converted, when it's seen on the command line.
"""

If flags are defined in different packages `__init__.py` files, how are they discovered prior to parsing the flags? For instance, assume a standard project:

module/__init__.py

```python
if __name__ == "__main__":
  FLAGS(argv)
  server.start()
```

And our server object uses a library called foo that defines a flag that is not imported until the server is actually invoked:

module/foo/__init__.py
```python
DEFINE_string('debug', None, 'Debug mode')
```

This will raise an error as the flag definition will not be known by flags yet. I'm probably missing something here - looking for guidance.

issue_title: Spec clarity: Versioning -- 64 bits vs 32 bits.
issue_body:
In all the places where version numbers are used in the protobuf specification, they are represented using 64 bits. 

However, the 'Versioning.md' document unambiguously states that version numbers are packed in 32 bits:

``
For efficiency, ONNX serializes the MAJOR, MINOR, and PATCH values as a bit-packed 32-bit integer; the most significant byte is the MAJOR component, the second most significant byte is the MINOR component, the least significant two bytes are the PATCH component.
``

This difference leads to confusion around what the correct encoding is, and needs to be straightened out. 

I see three options:

1. Using 64 bits to store the integer, but only using 32 bits. This is odd, but would require only that the documentation clearly explains the oddity.
2. We simply change the documentation to reflect packing of version numbers into 64 bits. Instead of one byte for each of the major/minor numbers, we would use two for each, and instead of two bytes for the PATCH component, we would use four.
3. Change the proto files to hold the version numbers in 32 bits. This would be a breaking change.

Option no. 1 would be the least impactful, but would perpetuate something rather odd.

So far, neither the IR versions or the operator set versions actually depends on the stated 32-bit encoding, so as long as no published models rely on the encoding described in 'Versioning.md,' going with option no. 2 would not impact anything, but I'm not sure whether we know that.

No. 3 seems to me the least desirable, since it's a breaking change to fix something rather trivial.

If variable bounds are set/changed after the initial solve, they new bound is not registered. Here's an example with the HS71 problem where the lower bound on x1 is changed from 0.1 to 1: 
```
from gekko import GEKKO
# Initialize Model
m = GEKKO(remote=False)
#initialize variables
x1,x2,x3,x4 = [m.Var() for i in range(4)]
#initial values
x1.value = 1
x2.value = 5
x3.value = 5
x4.value = 1
#lower bounds
x1.lower = .1
x2.lower = 1
x3.lower = 1
x4.lower = 1
#upper bounds
x1.upper = 5
x2.upper = 5
x3.upper = 5
x4.upper = 5
#Equations
m.Equation(x1*x2*x3*x4>=25)
m.Equation(x1**2+x2**2+x3**2+x4**2==40)
#Objective
m.Obj(x1*x4*(x1+x2+x3)+x3)
#Set global options
m.options.IMODE = 3 #steady state optimization
#Solve simulation
m.solve(disp=False) # solve on public server
print(m._variables)

#%% adjust problem and resolve
x1.LOWER = 1
m.solve(disp=False)
print(m._variables)
```
The output shows that the lower bound of 1 on x1 is not used:
```
[[0.7545087], [4.639369], [3.788569], [1.885132]]

Comment 1 by gjhigg...@gmail.com
The supported approach is to use the "IOMemory" store via the plugin mechanism. 

Below is shown the reasonably complete example on the wiki's IntroStore page (http://code.google.com/p/rdflib/wiki/IntroStore) that has been straightforwardly adapted to use IOMemory and is ready for copy'n'pasta. HTH

{{{
from rdflib import ConjunctiveGraph as Graph
from rdflib import plugin
from rdflib.store import Store, NO_STORE, VALID_STORE
from rdflib import Namespace
from rdflib import Literal
from rdflib import URIRef

default_graph_uri = "http://rdflib.net/rdfstore"
configString = ""

# Get the mysql plugin. You may have to install the python mysql libraries
store = plugin.get('IOMemory', Store)('rdfstore')

# Open previously created store, or create it if it doesn't exist yet
rt = store.open(configString,create=False)
if rt == NO_STORE:
    # There is no underlying MySQL infrastructure, create it
    store.open(configString,create=True)
else:
    assert rt == VALID_STORE,"There underlying store is corrupted"
    
# There is a store, use it
graph = Graph(store, identifier = URIRef(default_graph_uri))

print "Triples in graph before add: ", len(graph)

# Now we'll add some triples to the graph & commit the changes
rdflibns = Namespace('http://rdflib.net/test/')
graph.add((rdflibns['pic:1'], rdflibns['name'], Literal('Jane & Bob')))
graph.add((rdflibns['pic:2'], rdflibns['name'], Literal('Squirrel in Tree')))
graph.commit()

print "Triples in graph after add: ", len(graph)

# display the graph in RDF/XML
print graph.serialize() 
}}}

This doesn't appear to affect ListField, which does serialize into correct json format.  Also, this is most likely a problem with djangotoolbox, not django-nonrel, but I'm not sure where to submit issues for that project.

```py
from django.db import models
from djangotoolbox import fields
    
class Post(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=256)
    user = models.ForeignKey(User)
    content = models.TextField()
    tags = fields.SetField(models.CharField(max_length=128, blank=True), null=True, blank=True)
```

Calling dumpdata on a Post object results in json like this:

```py
{"pk": "4efb8096aae5710ad4000000",
 "model": "blog.post",
 "fields": {"tags": "set([u'home', u'features'])",
            "content":"foo",
            "user": "4ded8376aae5713406000010",
            "title": "My Title",
            "slug": "my-title"}
}
```

Since the json format doesn't have a set notation, it probably should just serialize to the same format as the ListField.
